subtitle: Journal-club style ICU update: PICO, key results, appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English.

script.heading: Intro
sentences: Welcome to Critical Care Daily for July 14, 2026.
Today we will review 3 recent ICU-relevant publications.
The format is quick listen first, then PICO, key results, critical appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English notes.
Use this as a morning triage tool, then open the source link for full-text appraisal before changing practice.

script.heading: Story 1 - The ARDS, Pneumonia, and Sepsis (APS) Consortium: Rationale, D
sentences: Story 1 is about The ARDS, Pneumonia, and Sepsis (APS) Consortium: Rationale, Design, and Feasibility of a National Platform for Phenotyping Critical Illness Syndromes..
This is a recent research article from Chest.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in critically ill adults with ARDS, pneumonia, and/or sepsis over 4 years, how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?
The first 1,000 participants were recruited ahead of schedule in less than 13 months.
Median age was 64 years, 75% received vasopressors, 50% received invasive mechanical ventilation, and 25% died in the hospital within 4 weeks of enrollment.

Biospecimen collection rates were high, with 99% of participants with blood, 98% with upper respiratory swabs, 37% with lower respiratory samples, 80% with urine, and 65% with gastrointestinal samples.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 2 - Determinants of Delayed Recovery of Consciousness After Analgo
sentences: Story 2 is about Determinants of Delayed Recovery of Consciousness After Analgosedation Discontinuation in the ICU: Insights From Patients With COVID-19 Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure..
This is a recent research article from Critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text., how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?
Our primary exposure variable was a time-weighted dose of analgosedative agents at benzodiazepine and/or propofol cessation; our primary outcome was time to RoC.
Seventy-three percent of patients had RoC before hospital discharge, yet 34% of patients achieving RoC did not do so within pharmacologically plausible sedative elimination times.
We estimated the latest expected RoC (LERoC) using models of sedation exposure that account for sedative-specific pharmacokinetics and critical illness.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 3 - Airway Occlusions to Measure Inspiratory Effort, Respiratory D
sentences: Story 3 is about Airway Occlusions to Measure Inspiratory Effort, Respiratory Drive, and Lung Mechanics During Noninvasive Ventilation..
This is a recent research article from American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in patients underwent two randomized 1-hour NIV sessions with oro-nasal and full-face masks after extubation, how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?

Predicted ΔPes agreed with observed ΔPes (oro-nasal bias -0.41 cm H2O, 95% limits of agreement -2.3 to 1.5; full-face 0.09, -2.9 to 3.1), and predicted ΔPL,dyn agreed with observed ΔPL,dyn (oro-nasal bias 0.03, -2.9 to 2.9; full-face -0.04, -4.3 to 4.2).
Ventilator-derived P0.1 did not precisely quantify respiratory drive, but values >2.7 cm H2O with the oro-nasal mask and >3 cm H2O with the full-face mask identified high drive with specificity >90%.
Plateau pressure was unstable in 78% (oro-nasal) and 90% (full-face) of patients.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Outro
sentences: That wraps up today's journal club style update.
Before applying any finding, check the full source, the absolute effect size, and your local ICU context.
For English practice, repeat one academic phrase from each article out loud.

articles.title: The ARDS, Pneumonia, and Sepsis (APS) Consortium: Rationale, Design, and Feasibility of a National Platform for Phenotyping Critical Illness Syndromes.
articles.summary: The APS Consortium Phenotyping Study is a multicenter longitudinal prospective observational cohort study aimed at enrolling 4,000 critically ill adults with ARDS, pneumonia, and/or sepsis over 4 years. Feasibility of the study was assessed by evaluating the first 1,000 participants in terms of recruitment pace, participant characteristics, biospecimen collection, and proportion with confirmed ARDS, pneumonia, and sepsis based on expert adjudication. The first 1,000 participants were recruited ahead of schedule in less than 13 months. Median age was 64 years, 75% received vasopressors, 50% received invasive mechanical ventilation, and 25% died in the hospital within 4 weeks of enrollment. Biospecimen collection rates were high, with 99% of participants with blood, 98% with upper respiratory swabs, 37% with lower respiratory samples, 80% with urine, and 65% with gastrointestinal samples.

Median age was 64 years, 75% received vasopressors, 50% received invasive mechanical ventilation, and 25% died in the hospital within 4 weeks of enrollment. Biospecimen collection rates were high, with 99% of participants with blood, 98% with upper respiratory swabs, 37%...

keyResults: The first 1,000 participants were recruited ahead of schedule in less than 13 months.
Median age was 64 years, 75% received vasopressors, 50% received invasive mechanical ventilation, and 25% died in the hospital within 4 weeks of enrollment.

Biospecimen collection rates were high, with 99% of participants with blood, 98% with upper respiratory swabs, 37% with lower respiratory samples, 80% with urine, and 65% with gastrointestinal samples.
Expert adjudication resulted in 40% classified with ARDS, 52% with pneumonia, and 89% with sepsis.
criticalAppraisal: Early evidence: useful for awareness, not enough alone to change practice.
articles.practiceImpact: Use as an awareness item; read the full article before applying it clinically.

articles.title: Determinants of Delayed Recovery of Consciousness After Analgosedation Discontinuation in the ICU: Insights From Patients With COVID-19 Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure.
articles.summary: Prolonged disorders of consciousness are common in critically ill patients receiving mechanical ventilation and are often attributed to prolonged sedative exposure in the setting of decreased drug clearance and/or reduced metabolism. Here, in a large sample of critically ill COVID patients obtained over a short period, we tested the assumption that prolonged unconsciousness following benzodiazepine and/or propofol sedation can be attributed to residual exposure. Our primary exposure variable was a time-weighted dose of analgosedative agents at benzodiazepine and/or propofol cessation; our primary outcome was time to RoC. Seventy-three percent of patients had RoC before hospital discharge, yet 34% of patients achieving RoC did not do so within pharmacologically plausible sedative elimination times. Further, we examine associations between clinical variables on time to recovery of consciousness (RoC) as a framework for the broader critically ill population.

pico.outcomes: We estimated the latest expected RoC (LERoC) using models of sedation exposure that account for sedative-specific pharmacokinetics and critical illness. Our primary exposure variable was a time-weighted dose of analgosedative agents at benzodiazepine and/or propofol cessation; our primary outcome was time to RoC. We estimated relative risks for late RoC...

keyResults: Our primary exposure variable was a time-weighted dose of analgosedative agents at benzodiazepine and/or propofol cessation; our primary outcome was time to RoC.
Seventy-three percent of patients had RoC before hospital discharge, yet 34% of patients achieving RoC did not do so within pharmacologically plausible sedative elimination times.
We estimated the latest expected RoC (LERoC) using models of sedation exposure that account for sedative-specific pharmacokinetics and critical illness.
We estimated relative risks for late RoC (i.e., after LERoC) via multinomial logistic regression.
criticalAppraisal: Early evidence: useful for awareness, not enough alone to change practice.

articles.title: Airway Occlusions to Measure Inspiratory Effort, Respiratory Drive, and Lung Mechanics During Noninvasive Ventilation.
articles.source: American journal of respiratory and critical care medicine
articles.summary: Predicted ΔPes agreed with observed ΔPes (oro-nasal bias -0.41 cm H2O, 95% limits of agreement -2.3 to 1.5; full-face 0.09, -2.9 to 3.1), and predicted ΔPL,dyn agreed with observed ΔPL,dyn (oro-nasal bias 0.03, -2.9 to 2.9; full-face -0.04, -4.3 to 4.2). Ventilator-derived P0.1 did not precisely quantify respiratory drive, but values >2.7 cm H2O with the oro-nasal mask and >3 cm H2O with the full-face mask identified high drive with specificity >90%. Plateau pressure was unstable in 78% (oro-nasal) and 90% (full-face) of patients. In intubated patients, occlusion maneuvers allow non-invasive assessment of inspiratory effort, respiratory drive and lung mechanics. To assess the feasibility of occlusion maneuvers during noninvasive ventilation (NIV).
